title_en: The Creation of the Heavens, Earth, and All That Is Between
title_id: Penciptaan Langit, Bumi, dan Segala yang Ada di Antaranya
content_id:
Ibn Kathir membuka al-Bidayah wal-Nihayah dengan narasi penciptaan sebagaimana ia ditetapkan dalam Al-Quran, Sunnah yang sahih, dan literatur tafsir awal, dimulai sebelum keberadaan langit dan bumi itu sendiri. Beliau menyajikan riwayat-riwayat yang menunjukkan bahwa Allah menciptakan Pena (al-qalam) sebelum segala sesuatu yang lain dan memerintahkannya untuk menulis semua yang akan terjadi hingga Hari Kiamat, dan bahwa 'Arsy dan Kursi termasuk di antara makhluk-makhluk yang paling awal diciptakan. Ibn Kathir mengutip hadis 'Imran ibn Husain yang tercatat dalam Sahih al-Bukhari, di mana Nabi memberitahukan bahwa Allah ada ketika tidak ada sesuatu pun yang lain, kemudian menciptakan 'Arsy di atas air, lalu menciptakan langit dan bumi. Beliau mencatat bahwa 'Arsy digambarkan dalam banyak ayat Al-Quran sebagai makhluk yang paling agung, meliputi seluruh ciptaan, dan bahwa Kursi, yang berbeda dari 'Arsy, meliputi langit dan bumi sebagaimana disebutkan dalam Ayat al-Kursi.

Penciptaan langit dan bumi dalam enam hari (ayyam) dibahas dengan ketepatan teologis yang menjadi ciri khas metodologi Athari Ibn Kathir. Beliau menetapkan dari Al-Quran bahwa enam hari itu adalah gambaran nyata dari tindakan-tindakan penciptaan yang berurutan, sambil juga mencatat diskusi para ulama tentang apakah ini adalah hari-hari sebagaimana manusia memahaminya atau periode waktu yang lebih panjang. Penciptaan bumi digambarkan terjadi dalam dua hari, diikuti oleh penyediaan rezeki, tumbuhan, dan gunung-gunungnya dalam empat hari, kemudian penciptaan tujuh langit dalam dua hari. Ibn Kathir mengaitkan kisah-kisah Al-Quran ini dengan prinsip bahwa Allah tidak merasa lelah oleh penciptaan, menolak segala perbandingan dengan kisah Alkitab tentang istirahat ilahi pada hari ketujuh. Beliau mengutip ayat Al-Quran: 'Dan Kami tidak merasa lelah dari penciptaan yang pertama, maka mengapa mereka ragu tentang penciptaan yang baru?'

Penciptaan para malaikat dan jin mendapat perlakuan rinci melalui riwayat-riwayat hadis yang tersedia. Nabi memberitahukan bahwa para malaikat diciptakan dari cahaya (nur), dan Ibn Kathir mencatat bahwa ini membedakan mereka secara kategoris dari manusia dan jin. Jin diciptakan dari api (nar), khususnya dari ujung nyala api, sebelum penciptaan Adam. Ibn Kathir mendokumentasikan materi Al-Quran dan hadis yang luas tentang para malaikat: jumlah mereka yang luar biasa banyak, berbagai fungsi mereka, malaikat-malaikat utama seperti Jibril, Mikail, Israfil, dan Malik penjaga Neraka, malaikat-malaikat kematian, dan malaikat-malaikat yang mencatat amal manusia. Beliau mengakui bahwa banyak dari yang dilaporkan tentang karakteristik spesifik dan jumlah malaikat berasal dari Isra'iliyyat dan harus diperlakukan dengan kehati-hatian daripada diterima atau ditolak tanpa bukti.

Ibn Kathir menutup bagian pembuka ini dengan mengartikulasikan pendekatan metodologisnya untuk keseluruhan karya. Beliau berkomitmen untuk menyajikan apa yang ditetapkan dalam Al-Quran dan hadis yang disahihkan sebagai pengetahuan yang pasti, untuk mengevaluasi riwayat-riwayat yang dinilai hasan atau dhaif sesuai dengan status epistemisnya yang tepat, dan untuk mengidentifikasi materi yang berasal dari Isra'iliyyat, yakni riwayat-riwayat yang ditransmisikan dari Ahli Kitab, dengan panduan bahwa materi tersebut tidak boleh dipercaya maupun ditolak tanpa koroborasi Al-Quran atau kenabian yang independen. Kejelasan metodologis ini, yang berakar pada ilmu hadis yang dikembangkan oleh para ulama tradisi klasik, membedakan al-Bidayah dari sejarah-sejarah universal terdahulu yang memasukkan Isra'iliyyat tanpa perangkat kritis. Bab-bab pembuka dengan demikian berfungsi baik sebagai fondasi teologis maupun sebagai demonstrasi prinsip-prinsip historiografi Ibn Kathir, yang akan beliau terapkan secara konsisten di seluruh empat belas jilid karya yang telah diselesaikan.
content_en:
Ibn Kathir opens al-Bidayah wan-Nihayah with the creation narrative as it is established in the Quran, the authentic Sunnah, and the early tafsir literature, beginning before the existence of the heavens and earth themselves. He presents narrations indicating that Allah created the Pen (al-qalam) before everything else and commanded it to write all that would come to pass until the Day of Judgment, and that the Throne ('arsh) and the Kursi were among the earliest of created things. Ibn Kathir cites the hadith of 'Imran ibn Husayn recorded in Sahih al-Bukhari, in which the Prophet informed that Allah existed when there was nothing else, and then created the Throne upon water, then created the heavens and earth. He notes that the Throne is described in numerous Quranic verses as the greatest of created things, encompassing all of creation, and that the Kursi, which is distinct from the Throne, encompasses the heavens and earth as described in Ayat al-Kursi.

The creation of the heavens and earth in six days (ayyam) is addressed with the theological precision characteristic of Ibn Kathir's Athari methodology. He establishes from the Quran that the six days are a genuine description of sequential creative acts, while also noting scholarly discussion on whether these are days as humans understand them or greater periods of time. The creation of the earth is described as occurring in two days, followed by the provision of its sustenance, vegetation, and mountains in four days, and then the creation of the seven heavens in two days. Ibn Kathir connects these Quranic accounts with the principle that Allah was not fatigued by creation, refuting any comparison with the Biblical account of divine rest on the seventh day. He cites the Quranic verse: 'And We did not tire from the first creation, so why would they be in doubt about a new creation?'

The creation of the angels and the jinn receives detailed treatment through the available hadith narrations. The Prophet informed that the angels were created from light (nur), and Ibn Kathir notes that this distinguishes them categorically from human beings and jinn. The jinn were created from fire (nar), specifically from the tip of the flame, before the creation of Adam. Ibn Kathir documents the extensive Quranic and hadith material on the angels: their tremendous number, their different functions, the major angels such as Jibril, Mika'il, Israfil, and Malik the keeper of Hell, the angels of death, and the angels who record human deeds. He acknowledges that much of what is reported about the specific characteristics and numbers of angels derives from Isra'iliyyat and should be treated with caution rather than accepted or rejected without evidence.

Ibn Kathir closes this opening section by articulating his methodological approach for the entire work. He commits to presenting what is established in the Quran and the authenticated hadith as certain knowledge, to evaluating reports graded as hasan or weak according to their appropriate epistemic status, and to identifying material derived from Isra'iliyyat, meaning narrations transmitted from the People of the Book, with the guidance that such material is neither to be believed nor rejected without independent Quranic or prophetic corroboration. This methodological clarity, rooted in the hadith sciences developed by the scholars of the classical tradition, distinguishes the Bidayah from earlier universal histories that incorporated Isra'iliyyat without critical apparatus. The opening chapters thus serve as both a theological foundation and a demonstration of Ibn Kathir's historiographical principles, which he would apply consistently across the fourteen volumes of the completed work.
content_ar: Ibn Kathir opens al-Bidayah wan-Nihayah with the creation narrative as ثبت in the القرآن الكريم, the authentic السنة, and the early التفسير literature, beginning قبل the existence of the heavens and earth themselves. He presents narrations indicating that الله created the Pen (al-qalam) قبل everything else and commanded it to write all that would come to pass حتى the يوم القيامة, and that the Throne ('arsh) and the Kursi were among the earliest of created things. Ibn Kathir cites the الحديث of 'Imran ibn Husayn recorded in صحيح البخاري, in which النبي ﷺ informed that الله existed عندما there was nothing else, and then created the Throne upon water, then created the heavens and earth. He notes that the Throne is described in numerous القرآني الآيات as the greatest of created things, encompassing all of creation, and that the Kursi, which is distinct from the Throne, encompasses the heavens and earth as described in Ayat al-Kursi. The creation of the heavens and earth in six days (ayyam) is addressed with the theological precision characteristic of Ibn Kathir's الأثري methodology. He establishes from the القرآن الكريم that the six days are a genuine description of sequential creative acts, بينما أيضاً noting العلمي discussion on whether these are days as humans understand them or greater periods of time. The creation of the earth is described as occurring in two days, followed by the provision of its sustenance, vegetation, and mountains in four days, and then the creation of the seven heavens in two days. Ibn Kathir connects these القرآني accounts with the principle that الله was not fatigued by creation, refuting any comparison with the Biblical account of divine rest on the سابعاً day. He cites the القرآني الآية: 'And We did not tire from the أولاً creation, لذا why would they be in doubt about a new creation?' The creation of the الملائكة and the الجن receives detailed treatment through the available الحديث narrations. النبي ﷺ informed that the الملائكة were created from light (nur), and Ibn Kathir notes that this distinguishes them categorically from human beings and الجن. The الجن were created from fire (nar), specifically from the tip of the flame, قبل the creation of آدم. Ibn Kathir documents the extensive القرآني and الحديث material on the الملائكة: their tremendous number, their different functions, the major الملائكة مثل جبريل ﷺ, Mika'il, Israfil, and مالك the keeper of النار, the الملائكة of الموت, and the الملائكة who record human deeds. He acknowledges that much of what is reported about the specific characteristics and numbers of الملائكة derives from Isra'iliyyat and should be treated with caution rather than accepted or rejected without evidence. Ibn Kathir closes this opening الفصل by articulating his methodological approach for the entire work. He commits to presenting what is established in the القرآن الكريم and the authenticated الحديث as certain العلم, to evaluating reports graded as ha
